questions.question: Which are the three top-level branches of the executive function taxonomy (Figure 13.1)? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The chapter organises EF into three branches: establishing & modifying rules (initiating, inhibiting, shifting, relating), contextual control (monitoring the environment), and working memory (maintenance + manipulation). Sensory integration and consolidation are not EF branches.

questions.question: Which features characterise the frontal disinhibition syndrome (ventral/medial PFC damage)? (Select all that apply)

options.C: Apathy and failure to initiate tasks
options.D: Failure to read social cues, profanity, aggression

questions.explanation: Frontal disinhibition syndrome (ventral/medial PFC) features unproductive movement, manic affect, social-cue failure, and acquired sociopathy in severe cases. Apathy and initiation failure characterise the dysexecutive syndrome from lateral PFC damage instead.

questions.question: Which statements about PFC connectivity are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.A: PFC connects with secondary, not primary, sensory cortices as a general rule
options.B: OFC is an exception — it receives input from primary taste, olfactory, and somatosensory cortices
options.C: PFC has broad, diverse, bidirectional connections — more than any other cortical region
options.D: Information returns from basal ganglia to PFC only indirectly via thalamus and substantia nigra
options.E: V1 projects directly to lateral PFC

questions.explanation: PFC connects with secondary cortices (e.g., inferior temporal cortex, not V1); OFC is the exception. PFC has the broadest connectivity of any cortical region. PFC projects directly to BG but receives only indirect feedback via thalamus and substantia nigra.

questions.question: Which are the three basal ganglia loops described in the lecture (Figure 13.7)? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The three basal ganglia loops are motor (primary/premotor/SMA → planning + execution), prefrontal (dlPFC → planning, decision making), and affective (ACC, OFC → emotion, mood, reward). There are no dedicated visual or auditory BG loops.

questions.question: Which features are characteristic of Lhermitte's environmental dependency syndrome? (Select all that apply)

options.D: Behaviour driven by environment, not internal goals

questions.explanation: Lhermitte (1986) described environmental dependency syndrome from anterior/medial frontal damage: patients show imitation behaviour, utilization behaviour (pour water, hammer when seen), with action driven by environmental affordances rather than internal goals. Spatial deficits are not part of the syndrome.

questions.question: Which statements about the rostral-caudal gradient in lateral PFC are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Posterior PFC handles concrete, motor-near rules
options.B: Anterior PFC handles abstract, goal-distant rules
options.C: Koechlin proposed a temporal abstraction cascade
options.D: Badre & D'Esposito proposed a policy abstraction hierarchy
options.E: Frontopolar cortex tracks goals/sub-goals and supports relational integration

questions.explanation: All five are correct. The gradient runs concrete-posterior to abstract-anterior; Koechlin's cascade frames it as temporal abstraction; Badre & D'Esposito frame it as policy abstraction; FPC sits at the apex for goal tracking, relational integration, and exploratory information-seeking.

questions.question: Which are challenges to the conflict-monitoring model of ACC? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Specificity problem — ACC also responds to correct feedback and rewards
options.B: ACC may track avoidance of mistakes, not just error detection
options.C: Fellows & Farah (2005): dorsal-ACC lesion patients have a normal Stroop effect
options.D: Monkey cingulate neurons did not increase activity under high response conflict
options.E: ACC has no connections to lateral PFC

questions.explanation: All except E are real challenges: ACC lacks specificity (responds to rewards/correct feedback), tracks mistake-avoidance, lesion patients show normal Stroop, and monkey cingulate units don't track response conflict. ACC clearly does connect with lateral PFC (e.g., Kerns et al. 2004).

questions.question: Which statements about the Wallis, Anderson & Miller (2001) study are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Monkeys followed either a matching or non-matching rule
options.B: Cues included juice reward (matching) and high tone (non-matching)
options.C: Single neurons fired selectively for the rule, not the cue or stimulus
options.D: Rule-selective neurons cluster along the principal sulcus
options.E: All rule coding was found in the orbitofrontal cortex

questions.explanation: The study used matching/non-matching rules signalled by juice or tone cues. Single-unit recordings in lateral PFC revealed rule-selective neurons (independent of cue and stimulus) clustered along the principal sulcus. Rule coding was lateral PFC, not OFC.

questions.question: Which statements about working memory are supported by the lecture's distributed network view? (Select all that apply)

options.A: WM content lives in the same sensory regions that originally processed it
options.B: PFC orchestrates via top-down signals but does not store content
options.C: Harrison & Tong (2009) decoded remembered orientation from V1-V4
options.D: Posterior damage causes content-specific WM deficits
options.E: PFC is the sole storage site for all working memory

questions.explanation: WM emerges from a distributed network: content stays in original sensory regions (Harrison & Tong 2009), PFC orchestrates without storing. Posterior damage causes content-specific deficits (e.g., spatial but not face WM); PFC damage impairs control across the board. PFC is not the sole store.

questions.question: Which findings characterise the dmPFC posterior-to-anterior gradient (Venkatraman et al. 2009; Taren et al. 2011)? (Select all that apply)

options.B: Middle dmPFC = decision-related control (face attractiveness, Pochon & Cohen)
options.C: Anterior dmPFC = strategy-related control (deviating from baseline strategy)
options.D: Anterior dmPFC connects to anterior dlPFC; posterior dmPFC connects to posterior dlPFC
options.E: The gradient runs anterior-concrete to posterior-abstract

questions.explanation: Venkatraman et al. (2009) mapped response/decision/strategy control onto a posterior-to-anterior gradient mirroring dlPFC; Taren et al. (2011) showed topographic medial-lateral connectivity (anterior-anterior, posterior-posterior) via fibre tracing, DTI, and resting-state fMRI. The gradient is least-abstract-posterior to most-abstract-anterior, not the reverse.
